EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The progression, first of a strong cold front that pushed across northern Colorado and then the surge of moisture that accompanied a decaying midlevel disturbance, resulted in heavy snow across the mountains of western Colorado. Snow began over the northern and central mountains 12  to 24 hours earlier than the rest of the zones as a result of the showers associated with the cold front. Additionally, the Upper Gunnison River Valley received moderate snow during the latter half of the event.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy snow fell across the area which led the Colorado Department of Transportation to close Douglas Pass several times during the event.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong Pacific trough and associated cold front pushed into western Colorado and produced heavy snow across the mountains. Additionally, steep mid-level lapse rates and moderate instability resulted in the development of some convective snow bands which enhanced snowfall rates and accumulations.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Generally 8 to 18 inches of snow fell across the area with up to 24 inches estimated at the Bear Town SNOTEL site.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: After additional heavy snow fell on an already unstable snowpack, a natural avalanche occurred southwest of Lake City and destroyed a home.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A natural avalanche released and hit a residence with 3 people inside along County Road 30, southwest of Lake City. The occupants were rescued and taken to a medical facility. The house was destroyed in the avalanche.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong Pacific trough and associated cold front pushed into western Colorado and produced heavy snow across the mountains. EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A midlevel disturbance dropped into the Four Corners area and progressed east over the San Juan Mountains. Enhanced lift aloft combined with modest moisture and favorable flow produced moderate snow over the southwest San Juans.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Generally 4 to 8 inches of snow fell across the area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A mild and moist atmospheric river event produced widespread precipitation across western Colorado with mountain snow and valley rain. Favorable upper level jet support and orographic flow resulted in significant snow over the northern and central mountains with moderate snow over the southern mountains.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Generally 10 to 20 inches of snow fell across the area with up to 24 inches estimated at the Molas Lake SNOTEL site.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Two shortwave disturbances associated with a broad upper level trough lifted across the Four Corners area. The subsequent ample moisture and enhanced orographic lift contributed to heavy snow across the San Juan Mountains.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Generally 10 to 20 inches of snow fell across the area with a peak estimated of 24 inches at the Columbus Basin SNOTEL site. Winds gusted up to 55 MPH.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Two shortwave disturbances associated with a broad upper level trough lifted across the Four Corners area. The subsequent ample moisture and enhanced orographic lift contributed to heavy snow across the San Juan Mountains.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Generally 5 to 11 inches of snow fell across the area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The unsettled, progressive weather pattern continued across western Colorado during the month of March as numerous disturbances moved through. As a result of the wetter than normal conditions the state of the drought improved across the region, most notably in the Four Corners which finally eradicated the remaining extreme (D3) and severe (D2) drought conditions.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The remaining sliver of extreme (D3) and severe (D2) drought conditions along the southern tier of the forecast zone improved to the moderate (D1) category by the end of the month. Elsewhere, the moderate drought conditions that were in place at the beginning of the month improved to the abnormally dry (D0) category.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Dense fog was observed at the Eagle County Regional Airport with visibility at or below a quarter mile for a couple hours.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A Pacific low and associated cold front pushed across the Great Basin and produced moderate to heavy snow over the mountains of western Colorado, as well as some lower valleys. Additionally, a tightening gradient aloft led to gusty winds at the surface and periods of blowing snow.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Generally 6 to 12 inches of snow fell across the area. Winds gusted up to 40 to 50 MPH. Colorado Highway 160 was closed for almost an hour during the storm between Hesperus and Mancos due to multiple vehicle slide-offs. Additionally, a state trooper was injured when a vehicle slid into his vehicle while he was working on a major accident.

CZ_NAME: GORE AND ELK MOUNTAINS/CENTRAL MOUNTAIN VALLEYS
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Moist and moderately fast southwest flow out ahead of and with the passage of a Pacific trough produced significant snow over the mountains of western Colorado. Some of the southern, central and northern valleys also received moderate snowfall. The presence of a strong subtropical jet aloft provided additional support as well as gusty winds at the surface which led to areas of blowing snow and reduced visibility.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Generally 4 to 7 inches of snow fell across the area with locally higher amounts ranging from 10 to 18 inches at the ski resorts. Winds gusted up to 45 MPH which led to areas of blowing and drifting snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A mid level Pacific trough progressed east into western Colorado. Ample moisture in the unsettled southwesterly flow produced moderate wet, dense snowfall across much of the higher terrain. Additionally, the presence of a strong upper level jet overhead resulted in gusty winds at the surface which led to blowing snow and reduced visibilities.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Around 9 inches of snow was estimated at the Columbine Pass SNOTEL site. Winds gusted up to 45 MPH.

The presence of a strong subtropical jet aloft provided additional support as well as gusty winds at the surface which led to areas of blowing snow and reduced visibility.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Generally 6 to 10.5 inches of snow fell across the area. Winds gusted up to 40 MPH.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Rapid clearing and very wet low levels in the wake of an atmospheric river event led to areas of dense fog in many of the lower valleys.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Dense fog was observed at the Rangley Airport with visibility at or below a quarter mile for several hours.

CZ_NAME: WEST JACKSON & WEST GRAND COUNTIES ABOVE 9000 FEET
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A moist Pacific storm system brought moderate to heavy snowfall to the north central mountains of Colorado.  In addition to the snow, strong wind gusts up to around 75 mph were measured above timberline.  Storm totals included:  16 inches at Lake Irene, 13 inches at Never Summer, 11.5 at Arapahoe Ridge, 11 inches at Buffalo Park and Loveland; 8 inches at Deadman Hill and Roach; with 4 to 7 inches elsewhere.

CZ_NAME: GORE AND ELK MOUNTAINS/CENTRAL MOUNTAIN VALLEYS
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Ample moisture and energy out ahead of and with the progression of a Pacific trough moving across the Great Basin brought widespread precipitation to western Colorado. Additionally, a secondary shortwave trough moved through in the southwesterly flow which enhanced orographic flow over the mountains.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Generally 6 to 12 inches of snow fell across the area with up to 14.4 inches estimated at the Ivanhoe SNOTEL site. Due to the hazardous road conditions, schools in Aspen School District 1 were cancelled on Friday, January 18th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Numerous winter disturbances moved across western Colorado during the month of January which led to wetter than normal conditions and cooler temperatures. As a result, the state of the drought improved across much of the region, most notably in the Four Corners which finally began to break out of the exceptional (D4) drought conditions.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The sliver of extreme (D3) drought conditions along the west-central border of the zone improved two categories to moderate (D1) conditions. The severe (D2) drought across the rest of the area also improved to the moderate category by the end of the month.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The sliver of extreme (D3) drought conditions along the southeast border of the zone improved to severe (D2) conditions. The severe drought across the rest of the area improved to the moderate (D1) category by the end of the month.

CZ_NAME: GORE AND ELK MOUNTAINS/CENTRAL MOUNTAIN VALLEYS
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The unsettled, progressive weather pattern continued across western Colorado during the month of February as numerous winter disturbances moved through. As a result of the wetter than normal conditions the state of the drought improved across much of the region, most notably in the Four Corners which finally eradicated the remaining exceptional (D4) drought conditions.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The extreme (D3) drought conditions across the southern border of the zone improved two categories to the moderate (D1) category. The severe (D2) conditions across the remainder of the zone also improved to the moderate category by the end of the month.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A deep Pacific trough infiltrated the western U.S. and pushed across the Great Basin. A quick moving shortwave trough was ejected out ahead of the main system and into western Colorado and the resulting combination of ample moisture, orographic lift, and additional upper level support from a 90 to 100 knot jet led to widespread precipitation across the region. The passage of a secondary shortwave kept showers going along the Continental Divide through Wednesday, January 16th. Moderate snow fell across the mountains and the southwest flow allowed the southern valleys to receive some good snowfall.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Generally 5 to 10 inches of snow fell across the area with an estimated 13 inches at the Park Reservoir SNOTEL site. Winds gusted up to 45 MPH.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An unstable snow pack in the San Juan Mountains and a group of back country skiers triggered an avalanche in the Upper Senator Beck Basin, northwest of Red Mountain Pass in southwest Colorado.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A group of back country skiers participating in an avalanche safety course triggered an avalanche on the afternoon of January 5. All six members of the group were caught in the avalanche. The avalanche released a second avalanche sympathetically. The second avalanche ran over the debris pile of the first slide. One member of the group was buried in the debris of the two avalanches. The rest of the group located him and extricated him from under at least 8 feet of snow. They tried to revive him on site, but were unsuccessful. The rest of the group made their way to Highway 550 with the help of San Juan County Search and Rescue, Ouray Mountain Rescue, La Plata County Search and Rescue, Careflight, and the Colorado Avalanche Information Center.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low level moisture from melting snow, combined with clear skies, lead to areas of dense fog in the lower valleys of west-central Colorado.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: NWS employees reported dense fog in the vicinity of Fruita, the Redlands and across Orchard Mesa in the Grand Valley. Visibility was restricted to less than a quarter mile for several hours.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A deep Pacific trough infiltrated the western U.S. and pushed across the Great Basin. A quick moving shortwave trough was ejected out ahead of the main system and into western Colorado and the resulting combination of ample moisture, orographic lift, and additional upper level support from a 90 to 100 knot jet led to widespread precipitation across the region. EVENT_NARRATIVE: Generally 3 to 6 inches of snow fell across the area with up to 8 inches reported in the surrounding foothills. Snow showers produced difficult driving conditions which led to Durango School District 9-R cancelling all school and school-related activities Wednesday morning, January 16th. Additionally, the La Plata County government closed their offices for the day. The wet, heavy snow also resulted in power outages for 700 La Plata Electric customers in the Hermosa area.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Generally 6 to 12 inches of snow fell across the area with a report of 13 inches 7 miles northeast of Sawpit and a report of close to 16 inches south-southwest of Telluride.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Ample moisture and energy out ahead of and with the progression of a Pacific trough moving across the Great Basin brought widespread precipitation to western Colorado. Additionally, a secondary shortwave trough moved through in the southwesterly flow which enhanced orographic flow over the mountains.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Anywhere from 9 to 20 inches of snow fell across the area with most amounts in the 12 to 16 inch range. The Department of Transportation closed 44 miles of Colorado Highway 550, the main highway to get from Montrose to Durango and areas south, due to snowfall and natural avalanches crossing the road. The closure included all three mountain passes, Coal Bank, Molas and Red Mountain, and it started during the early morning hours on Friday, January 18th and the road did not reopen until Saturday evening, January 19th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong winds and snow moved in from the west across East Central Colorado during the morning.  The strong winds, combined with periods of moderate to heavy snow, produced visibilities of less than a quarter mile from the morning into the afternoon.  For Yuma County, wind gusts were just below criteria until the last couple hours of the event when gusts of 35-40 MPH during the last couple hours of the event.  Up to three inches of snow fell in Kit Carson and Yuma Counties, with only an inch or so in Cheyenne County.  By the end of the event I-70 was closed across Kit Carson County, possibly even further west.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: White-out conditions reported in Kit Carson.  Similar conditions reported in parts of Kit Carson and Wallace counties.

Some of the higher reported storm totals with this event included six to ten inches of snow near Alamosa, Maysville, Cascade, Texas Creek, Westcliffe, Woodland Park, Wetmore, Colorado City, Monarch Pass, Palmer Lake, Climax, Walsenburg, Divide, Monte Vista, Wolf Creek Pass, Peyton and the Air Force Academy. Eleven to twelve inches of snow was measured near Monument, Black Forest and the Spanish Peaks. 13 to 14 inches of snow graced the communities of Beulah and Rye, while 18 inches of snow impacted locations near San Isabel.

A deep Pacific trough infiltrated the western U.S. and pushed across the Great Basin. A quick moving shortwave trough was ejected out ahead of the main system and into western Colorado and the resulting combination of ample moisture, orographic lift, and additional upper level support from a 90 to 100 knot jet led to widespread precipitation across the region. The passage of a secondary shortwave kept showers going along the Continental Divide through Wednesday, January 16th. Moderate snow fell across the mountains and the southwest flow allowed the southern valleys to receive some good snowfall.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Generally 6 to 10 inches of snow fell across the area with locally higher amounts exceeding a foot at the Tower SNOTEL site.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A quick moving Pacific disturbance dropped into western Colorado from the northwest and produced moderate to heavy snowfall over the northern valleys and mountains. Favorable orographics allowed snow showers to persist along the Continental Divide for an additional 12 hours or so longer than areas further west.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Generally 3 to 6 inches of snow fell across the area. Winds gusted up to 35 MPH.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A passing storm system generated accumulating snow and gusty winds, primarily over sections of Chaffee, Custer, El Paso, Mineral, Otero and Pueblo counties.  Six to eight of snow visited locations near the Air Force Academy, Wolf Creek Pass, Westcliffe, Monument, Beulah, Rye, La Junta and Black Forest.  Eleven inches of snow impacted Monarch Pass. In addition, winds gusting to around 60 mph at times were noted near Peterson AFB, Blende, Fountain and Truckton.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A winter storm system brought a mix of strong winds with pockets of moderate to heavy snow to the southern Front Range Foothills and Palmer Divide; with blizzard conditions along I-70 east of Aurora.  Interstate 70 was closed in the morning through early afternoon on the 22nd, from the exit at Denver International Airport to the Kansas state line.  Parts of major highways including I-25 south of Denver toward Monument and Highway 24 were also closed for several hours.  Numerous accidents along I-70 were reported due to strong winds and low visibility of a quarter mile or less.  Strong northerly winds gusting from 45 to 55 mph were observed.  The heaviest snowfall occurred in the southern Front Range Foothills and the Palmer Divide south of Denver.  Storm totals in those areas included:  15 inches at Shaffers Crossing; 10 inches in Conifer; 9 inches near Ponderosa Park with 6.5 inches in Brookvale.  In addition, 6 inches of snowfall was observed in Agate with 5.5 inches at Castle Pines.  Elsewhere, lesser amounts from 1 to 4 inches were reported.

An extended period of bitter cold temperatures caused an ice jam to form on the Fryingpan and Roaring Fork Rivers in portions of Pitkin, Eagle and Garfield counties. Minor flooding occurred along the rivers with elevated river flows peaking during the early afternoon hours each day.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Emergency Manager in Pitkin County reported an Ice Jam that was breaking up along the Roaring Fork River with large debris and chunks of ice flow downstream. River gauges along the Roaring Fork River showed sudden rises from less than 1 foot to 1.5 feet and CFS increases from 7.33 CFS to near 45 CFS around midday on January 3rd and again on January 4th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An extended period of bitter cold temperatures caused an ice jam to form on the Fryingpan and Roaring Fork Rivers in portions of Pitkin, Eagle and Garfield counties. Minor flooding occurred along the rivers with elevated river flows peaking during the early afternoon hours each day.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Roaring Fork Conservancy District reported an Ice Jam that was breaking up along the Fryingpan River with large debris and chunks of ice flow downstream. The river peaked around midday on January 3rd and again on January 4th.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Emergency Managers and the Roaring Forking Conservancy District reported Ice Jams that were breaking up along the Roaring Fork and Fryingpan Rivers with large debris and chunks of ice flow downstream. River gauges along the Roaring Fork River showed sudden rises from less than 1 foot to 1.5 feet and CFS increases from 7.33 CFS to near 45 CFS around midday on January 3rd and again on January 4th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A significant storm moved across Cheyenne County Colorado and Wallace County Kansas on the evening of June 21, 2019. This storm produced powerful wind gusts and hail ranging from quarter size to tennis ball size. Sporadic wind damage occurred with this storm along with damage associated with wind-driven hail.

An upper level trough moved into western Colorado from the west and pulled in a tap of subtropical moisture with it from the southwest. This resulted in moderate to heavy snow showers at times for much of the valleys and mountains. After a brief lull on the night of Sunday, January 6th showers redeveloped along the Continental Divide the morning of Monday the 7th as a weak shortwave trough moved through.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Generally 6 to 10 inches of snow fell across the area with an estimated 8 to 10 inches on Baxter Pass. Winds gusted up to 25 MPH with a peak gust close to 50 MPH at Douglas Pass.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A high based heavy rain shower passed through the central parts of Prowers County on the evening of Saturday, April 20th. Sub-cloud layer dry air was in place allowing for enhanced downdraft activity. The shower, as it passed over KLAA producing an 82 MPH wind gust. Subsequently a severe thunderstorm was issued as winds blew to 70 MPH at the Prowers County EOC.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Lamar Airport, KLAA, gusted to 81 MPH as a strong rain shower passed overhead. No lightning was detected with the shower.

CZ_NAME: EASTERN LAKE COUNTY / WESTERN MOSQUITO RANGE ABOVE 11000 FT
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A passing storm system generated heavy snow at times in combination with gusty winds.  Some of the higher reported snow totals with this storm included six inches of snow near the communities of Beulah, Palmer Lake, Rye and Walsenburg.  Seven inches of snow grace Wetmore, while ten inches of snow was measured near Fort Garland.

A mid level Pacific trough progressed east into western Colorado. Ample moisture in the unsettled southwesterly flow produced moderate wet, dense snowfall across much of the higher terrain. Additionally, the presence of a strong upper level jet overhead resulted in gusty winds at the surface which led to blowing snow and reduced visibilities.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Generally 4 to 8 inches of snow fell across the area with up to 10 inches estimated at the Schofield Pass SNOTEL site.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A slow moving snow storm generated widespread snow, heavy at times over many portions of southern Colorado.  Some of the higher reported snow totals with this event included six inches of snow near Creede, Monument, Florissant, Palmer Lake and Rye.  Seven inches of snow graced locations near Buena Vista, Cascade, Hillside, Rosita, Westcliffe, Colorado City, Texas Creek and Beulah.  Eight inches of snow was observed near South Fork, Colorado Springs and Manitou Springs.  Locations near the Spanish Peaks, Black Forest and San Isabel recorded nine to ten inches of snow with this event, while Wolf Creek Pass was inundated with 40 inches of snow with this storm system.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: After midnight a band of moderate to heavy snow moved east over East Central Colorado.  The snow lasted until late morning before coming to and end.  Snowfall totals up to eight inches were reported, with the highest amount reported south of Burlington.  ||After sunrise north winds became gusty, causing blizzard conditions to occur in Cheyenne County.  Whiteout conditions were reported in Cheyenne Wells during this time.  Near blizzard conditions were reported in Kit Carson County where the visibility fell to a half mile or less as the winds increased, then improved as the winds slowed.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Whiteout conditions were reported in Cheyenne Wells.  Snowfall amounts ranged from six to seven inches across the county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Accelerated snowmelt due to above normal temperatures produced high and fast flow on the Green River. A man died from a recreational accident caused by the dangerous water conditions in Dinosaur National Monument.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A raft carrying several people down the Green River in Dinosaur National Monument flipped when it entered the rapids at Triplet Falls and became stuck on one of the rocks. Many of the passengers were ejected into the water and one man later died after he was pulled out of the river.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A lightning strike ignited a wildfire about 10 miles northeast of Durango.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The 441 Fire began in mid July due to lightning about 10 miles northeast of Durango and grew to 325 acres before it was contained in early August.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A wildfire was ignited, most likely by a lightning strike, 5.5 miles south-southwest of Rangely.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Park Fire began in mid July, most likely due to lightning, about 5.5 miles south-southwest of Rangely. It grew to 157 acres before it was contained a couple of days later.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A lightning strike ignited a wildfire about 11 miles south of Rifle.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Middle Mamm Fire began at the end of July due to lightning about 11 miles south of Rifle and grew to 1,235 acres before it was contained in early November.

A mid level Pacific trough progressed east into western Colorado. Ample moisture in the unsettled southwesterly flow produced moderate wet, dense snowfall across much of the higher terrain. Additionally, the presence of a strong upper level jet overhead resulted in gusty winds at the surface which led to blowing snow and reduced visibilities.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Generally 4 to 8 inches of snow fell across the area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A slow moving snow storm generated widespread snow, heavy at times over many portions of southern Colorado. Seven inches of snow graced locations near Buena Vista, Cascade, Hillside, Rosita, Westcliffe, Colorado City, Texas Creek and Beulah.  Eight inches of snow was observed near South Fork, Colorado Springs and Manitou Springs.  Locations near the Spanish Peaks, Black Forest and San Isabel recorded nine to ten inches of snow with this event, while Wolf Creek Pass was inundated with 40 inches of snow with this storm system.

CZ_NAME: JEFFERSON & W DOUGLAS COUNTIES ABOVE 6000 FEET / GILPIN / CLEAR CREEK / NE PARK COUNTIES BELOW 9000 FEET
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A winter storm system brought a mix of strong winds with pockets of moderate to heavy snow to the southern Front Range Foothills and Palmer Divide; with blizzard conditions along I-70 east of Aurora.  Interstate 70 was closed in the morning through early afternoon on the 22nd, from the exit at Denver International Airport to the Kansas state line.  Parts of major highways including I-25 south of Denver toward Monument and Highway 24 were also closed for several hours.  Numerous accidents along I-70 were reported due to strong winds and low visibility of a quarter mile or less.  Strong northerly winds gusting from 45 to 55 mph were observed.  The heaviest snowfall occurred in the southern Front Range Foothills and the Palmer Divide south of Denver.  Storm totals in those areas included:  15 inches at Shaffers Crossing; 10 inches in Conifer; 9 inches near Ponderosa Park with 6.5 inches in Brookvale.  In addition, 6 inches of snowfall was observed in Agate with 5.5 inches at Castle Pines.  Elsewhere, lesser amounts from 1 to 4 inches were reported.

A strong westerly flow aloft ushered a prolonged period of heavy snow and strong winds into the north central mountains of Colorado.  The combination of snow and wind resulted in a high avalanche danger.  An avalanche occurred along Interstate 70 between Frisco and Copper Mountain late in the afternoon on the 3rd.  Several vehicles were trapped by the snow as it swept across the interstate.  Another avalanche occurred near the same area earlier in the day.  No injuries were reported and the vehicles were able to pull out of the snow field.  Large stretch of I-70 now closed through the mountains.  On the 5th, both directions of Interstate 70 between Herman Gulch and Silverthorne were closed for 9 hours for avalanche mitigation work.  One of the avalanches  brought down more snow than expected and covered the westbound lanes with 15 feet of snow and the eastbound lanes with 8 feet.   ||Storm totals for the 3-day event included:  35 inches at Mt Audubon; 32.5 near Mt Zirkel; 27.5 inches near Hoosier Pass, Longs Peak, Loveland Pass; 26 inches near Berthoud Falls, Climax, Glendevey and Silverthorne; 25.5 inches near Frisco; 22.5 inches near Guanella Pass; 20 inches near Cameron Pass; 19 inches at Brainard Lake; 16.5 inches near Ward; 15 inches near Alma, Eldora and Meeker Park.||In the Front Range Foothills, storm totals included:  17 inches near Estes Park; 11 inches near Georgetown and Nederland; 10 inches near Virginia Dale; 8.5 inches near Buckhorn Mountain and Glen Haven.  Along the I-25 corridor and adjacent plains, storm totals included:  8.5 inches near  Fort Collins;  8 inches near Elizabeth and Nunn;  7.5 inches near Loveland; 7 inches near Wellington; with 2 to 6 inches elsewhere.
A severe thunderstorm moving out of the Front Range produce one-inch hail near the Broadmoor on Friday, March 29.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Large hail reported at the intersection of Circle Drive and Fountain Blvd in SW Colorado Springs.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong winter storm dumped 16 to 30 inches of new snow over the Southwest San Juan Mountains on top of an already unstable snowpack. A backcountry skier triggered an avalanche after diverting from the Matterhorn Nordic Trail north of Trout Lake, near Lizard Head Pass.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A backcountry skier was skiing up a mountain near the Matterhorn Nordic Trail system north of Trout Lake, near Lizard Head Pass, after a recent winter storm brought new snow in excess of 2 feet to the area. The new snow fell on top of an unstable snowpack from earlier winter storms. The avalanche was triggered by the skier going off course. It was a soft slab avalanche that broke around 300 feet wide and ran approximately 600 vertical feet. It released on a south-facing slope at around 11,250 feet. After the avalanche, the skier was buried approximately 3 feet deep and he did not survive.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The progression, first of a strong cold front that pushed across northern Colorado and then the surge of moisture that accompanied a decaying midlevel disturbance, resulted in heavy snow across the mountains of western Colorado. Snow began over the northern and central mountains 12  to 24 hours earlier than the rest of the zones as a result of the showers associated with the cold front. Additionally, the Upper Gunnison River Valley received moderate snow during the latter half of the event.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Both CoCoRaHS and cooperative weather observers near the Craig and Hayden areas reported 7 to 9 inches of snow fell.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong winter storm dumped 8 to 16 inches of new snow with locally higher amounts exceeding 2 feet over the West Elk Mountains on top of an already unstable snowpack. As two people cleared snow from a low-angled roof south of Crested Butte, the snow avalanched. That same storm produced 8 to 13 inches of new snow across the Gore and Elk Mountains which resulted in a natural avalanche in Castle Creek Valley south of Aspen.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Two people were clearing snow from a low-angled roof south of Crested Butte when the snow avalanched. Both individuals were caught and buried for around 10 minutes until someone noticed the avalanche. After emergency services arrived, the first person was dug out and survived. The second person was buried more deeply and when they were located by probing, they were not breathing. The two individuals were buried for approximately 20 to 30 minutes.

CZ_NAME: N DOUGLAS COUNTY BELOW 6000 FEET / DENVER / W ADAMS & ARAPAHOE COUNTIES / E BROOMFIELD COUNTY
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A winter storm system brought a mix of strong winds with pockets of moderate to heavy snow to the southern Front Range Foothills and Palmer Divide; with blizzard conditions along I-70 east of Aurora.  Interstate 70 was closed in the morning through early afternoon on the 22nd, from the exit at Denver International Airport to the Kansas state line.  Parts of major highways including I-25 south of Denver toward Monument and Highway 24 were also closed for several hours.  Numerous accidents along I-70 were reported due to strong winds and low visibility of a quarter mile or less.  Strong northerly winds gusting from 45 to 55 mph were observed.  The heaviest snowfall occurred in the southern Front Range Foothills and the Palmer Divide south of Denver.  Storm totals in those areas included:  15 inches at Shaffers Crossing; 10 inches in Conifer; 9 inches near Ponderosa Park with 6.5 inches in Brookvale.  In addition, 6 inches of snowfall was observed in Agate with 5.5 inches at Castle Pines.  Elsewhere, lesser amounts from 1 to 4 inches were reported.

CZ_NAME: BOULDER & JEFFERSON COUNTIES BELOW 6000 FEET / W BROOMFIELD COUNTY
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong westerly flow aloft ushered a prolonged period of heavy snow and strong winds into the north central mountains of Colorado.  The combination of snow and wind resulted in a high avalanche danger.  An avalanche occurred along Interstate 70 between Frisco and Copper Mountain late in the afternoon on the 3rd.  Several vehicles were trapped by the snow as it swept across the interstate.  Another avalanche occurred near the same area earlier in the day.  No injuries were reported and the vehicles were able to pull out of the snow field.  Large stretch of I-70 now closed through the mountains.  On the 5th, both directions of Interstate 70 between Herman Gulch and Silverthorne were closed for 9 hours for avalanche mitigation work.  One of the avalanches  brought down more snow than expected and covered the westbound lanes with 15 feet of snow and the eastbound lanes with 8 feet.   ||Storm totals for the 3-day event included:  35 inches at Mt Audubon; 32.5 near Mt Zirkel; 27.5 inches near Hoosier Pass, Longs Peak, Loveland Pass; 26 inches near Berthoud Falls, Climax, Glendevey and Silverthorne; 25.5 inches near Frisco; 22.5 inches near Guanella Pass; 20 inches near Cameron Pass; 19 inches at Brainard Lake; 16.5 inches near Ward; 15 inches near Alma, Eldora and Meeker Park.||In the Front Range Foothills, storm totals included:  17 inches near Estes Park; 11 inches near Georgetown and Nederland; 10 inches near Virginia Dale; 8.5 inches near Buckhorn Mountain and Glen Haven.  Along the I-25 corridor and adjacent plains, storm totals included:  8.5 inches near  Fort Collins;  8 inches near Elizabeth and Nunn;  7.5 inches near Loveland; 7 inches near Wellington; with 2 to 6 inches elsewhere.

CZ_NAME: JEFFERSON & W DOUGLAS COUNTIES ABOVE 6000 FEET / GILPIN / CLEAR CREEK / NE PARK COUNTIES BELOW 9000 FEET
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upslope snow event produced heavy snow in the southern Front Range Foothills and Palmer Divide south of Denver, with light to moderate snowfall elsewhere.  In the Front Range Foothills and Palmer Divide storm totals included:  18 inches at Schaffers Crossing; 16.5 inches, 3 miles south of Tiny Town; 15.5 inches in Pinecliffe; 14 inches in Crescent Village; 13.5 inches near Aspen Park; 13 inches, 11 miles southeast of Arapahoe Park; 12.5 inches near Genesee and Kittredge; 12 inches near Perry Park;  11 inches near Conifer; with 6 to 10 inches elsewhere.  Across the western and southern suburbs of Denver, storm totals included:  7.5 inches near Centennial; 6 inches in Louisville and Westminster, 5.5 inches in Federal Heights; 5 inches in Broomfield; with 2 to 4 inches elsewhere.

CZ_NAME: WEST JACKSON & WEST GRAND COUNTIES ABOVE 9000 FEET
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A prolonged period of heavy snowfall during the first week of March produced historic avalanche conditions across the northern and central mountains of Colorado.  Mountain highways including a large section of I-70 was closed on the 7th due to high to extreme avalanche danger.   The snow was heaviest during the overnight hours when a concentrated band of heavy snow remained stationary over a large stretch of the  Interstate 70 corridor, producing snowfall rates up to 2 inches per hour.  Storm totals included:  24 inches near Mount Zirkel; 17 inches near Loveland Pass; 15.5 inches near Berthoud Falls; 15 inches near Silverthorne; 14.5 inches near Climax; 13 inches near Cameron Pass, Glendevey, Longs Peak and Mount Audubon; 12 inches near Alma, Gould and Guanella Pass; 11 inches near Frisco; with 6 to 10 inches elsewhere.||An avalanche that ruptured a natural gas line behind the Conoco station at Copper Mountain; and several other avalanches led to the closure of I-70 in both directions between Copper Mountain and Vail.  The ruptured gas line created a power outage at the Eisenhower Tunnel.  Colorado Department of Transportation (CDOT) crews were escorting 100 vehicles through the tunnel when the outage occurred.  Interstate 70 between Silverthorne and Empire Junction was closed for about three hours in the area for avalanche mitigation.  Arapahoe Basin ski area did not open on the 7th because of high avalanche danger on nearby Loveland Pass.  The Colorado Avalanche Information Center deemed several of the backcountry zones EXTREME (5 of 5), including Vail and Summit County.  This was the first time this designation has been used since they converted to a ten-zone forecast format back in 2006. The warning comes after multiple avalanches over the previous weekend and an avalanche that has shut down Interstate 70 between Frisco and Vail early on the morning of the 7th.

CZ_NAME: N DOUGLAS COUNTY BELOW 6000 FEET / DENVER / W ADAMS & ARAPAHOE COUNTIES / E BROOMFIELD COUNTY
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A rapidly intensifying storm system or bomb cyclone brought hurricane strength winds to the northeast plains of Colorado, along with moderate to heavy snowfall.  Peak wind gusts ranged from 60 to 80 mph. Thundersnow produced snowfall rates up to 3 inches per hour just west of Denver.   Two records were established at Denver International Airport.  A record non-thunderstorm wind gust of 80 mph broke the previous record of 63 mph set in Match 13, 2009.  A record low sea level pressure of 979.01 MB broke the previous record of 979.9 MB set on February 25, 1998.  Snowfall amounts ranged from 2 to 6 inches near the base of the foothills, with up to 20 inches in the mountains and foothills.  Along the Palmer Divide east of I-25 and the northeast plains of Colorado, storm totals ranged from 4 to 10 inches.||Widespread outages, multi-vehicle accidents and road closures prompted the governor to declare a state of emergency which activated the Colorado National Guard to assist state and local authorities in rescuing hundreds of stranded motorists.  Disaster declarations included the following counties and cities:  Adams, Arapahoe, Douglas, Elbert counties, as well as the city of Aurora.   Multiple accidents included a 100-car pileup on Interstate 25, from MM 285 to the WY border; with another 100-car accident south of Denver toward Monument. A state trooper was struck and killed on Interstate 76.  He was at the scene of a vehicle off the road near Roggen when another car lost control and struck him.  Road closures included:  Interstate 70 from Denver Airport to Limon, and from Golden to Idaho Springs;  I-25 from South Denver to Monument Hill;  I-76 closed from northeast Denver to Nebraska.||Nearly 1400 flights in and out of Denver International Airport that were canceled due to the blizzard.  Five thousand passengers were stranded at the airport.  The number of people who lost power during the storm totaled 445,000.  Xcel energy called over 500 employees work to restore power with additional 300 out-of-state workers on their way to assist.  Approximately 85,000 people, mostly in the Denver area, were without electricity the following day.  At least 335 vehicles were stuck and abandoned on Interstate 25 near Larkspur.  In Denver, 250 vehicles were abandoned during the storm.  School buses were used to rescue stranded drivers.  At least 33 public school districts were closed on the 13 and 14th.  Warming centers and shelters opened area wide.

Peak wind gusts ranged from 60 to 80 mph. Thundersnow produced snowfall rates up to 3 inches per hour just west of Denver.   Two records were established at Denver International Airport.  A record non-thunderstorm wind gust of 80 mph broke the previous record of 63 mph set in Match 13, 2009.  A record low sea level pressure of 979.01 MB broke the previous record of 979.9 MB set on February 25, 1998.  Snowfall amounts ranged from 2 to 6 inches near the base of the foothills, with up to 20 inches in the mountains and foothills.  Along the Palmer Divide east of I-25 and the northeast plains of Colorado, storm totals ranged from 4 to 10 inches.||Widespread outages, multi-vehicle accidents and road closures prompted the governor to declare a state of emergency which activated the Colorado National Guard to assist state and local authorities in rescuing hundreds of stranded motorists.  Disaster declarations included the following counties and cities:  Adams, Arapahoe, Douglas, Elbert counties, as well as the city of Aurora.   Multiple accidents included a 100-car pileup on Interstate 25, from MM 285 to the WY border; with another 100-car accident south of Denver toward Monument. A state trooper was struck and killed on Interstate 76.  He was at the scene of a vehicle off the road near Roggen when another car lost control and struck him.  Road closures included:  Interstate 70 from Denver Airport to Limon, and from Golden to Idaho Springs;  I-25 from South Denver to Monument Hill;  I-76 closed from northeast Denver to Nebraska.||Nearly 1400 flights in and out of Denver International Airport that were canceled due to the blizzard.  Five thousand passengers were stranded at the airport.  The number of people who lost power during the storm totaled 445,000.  Xcel energy called over 500 employees work to restore power with additional 300 out-of-state workers on their way to assist.  Approximately 85,000 people, mostly in the Denver area, were without electricity the following day.  At least 335 vehicles were stuck and abandoned on Interstate 25 near Larkspur.  In Denver, 250 vehicles were abandoned during the storm.  School buses were used to rescue stranded drivers.  At least 33 public school districts were closed on the 13 and 14th.  Warming centers and shelters opened area wide.

CZ_NAME: S & SE GRAND / W CENTRAL & SW BOULDER / GILPIN / CLEAR CREEK / SUMMIT / N & W PARK COUNTIES ABOVE 9000 FEET
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A prolonged period of heavy snowfall during the first week of March produced historic avalanche conditions across the northern and central mountains of Colorado.  Mountain highways including a large section of I-70 was closed on the 7th due to high to extreme avalanche danger.   The snow was heaviest during the overnight hours when a concentrated band of heavy snow remained stationary over a large stretch of the  Interstate 70 corridor, producing snowfall rates up to 2 inches per hour.  Storm totals included:  24 inches near Mount Zirkel; 17 inches near Loveland Pass; 15.5 inches near Berthoud Falls; 15 inches near Silverthorne; 14.5 inches near Climax; 13 inches near Cameron Pass, Glendevey, Longs Peak and Mount Audubon; 12 inches near Alma, Gould and Guanella Pass; 11 inches near Frisco; with 6 to 10 inches elsewhere.||An avalanche that ruptured a natural gas line behind the Conoco station at Copper Mountain; and several other avalanches led to the closure of I-70 in both directions between Copper Mountain and Vail.  The ruptured gas line created a power outage at the Eisenhower Tunnel.  Colorado Department of Transportation (CDOT) crews were escorting 100 vehicles through the tunnel when the outage occurred.  Interstate 70 between Silverthorne and Empire Junction was closed for about three hours in the area for avalanche mitigation.  Arapahoe Basin ski area did not open on the 7th because of high avalanche danger on nearby Loveland Pass.  The Colorado Avalanche Information Center deemed several of the backcountry zones EXTREME (5 of 5), including Vail and Summit County.  This was the first time this designation has been used since they converted to a ten-zone forecast format back in 2006. The warning comes after multiple avalanches over the previous weekend and an avalanche that has shut down Interstate 70 between Frisco and Vail early on the morning of the 7th.

A winter storm system brought a mix of strong winds with pockets of moderate to heavy snow to the southern Front Range Foothills and Palmer Divide; with blizzard conditions along I-70 east of Aurora.  Interstate 70 was closed in the morning through early afternoon on the 22nd, from the exit at Denver International Airport to the Kansas state line.  Parts of major highways including I-25 south of Denver toward Monument and Highway 24 were also closed for several hours.  Numerous accidents along I-70 were reported due to strong winds and low visibility of a quarter mile or less.  Strong northerly winds gusting from 45 to 55 mph were observed.  The heaviest snowfall occurred in the southern Front Range Foothills and the Palmer Divide south of Denver.  Storm totals in those areas included:  15 inches at Shaffers Crossing; 10 inches in Conifer; 9 inches near Ponderosa Park with 6.5 inches in Brookvale.  In addition, 6 inches of snowfall was observed in Agate with 5.5 inches at Castle Pines.  Elsewhere, lesser amounts from 1 to 4 inches were reported.

The progression, first of a strong cold front that pushed across northern Colorado and then the surge of moisture that accompanied a decaying midlevel disturbance, resulted in heavy snow across the mountains of western Colorado. Snow began over the northern and central mountains 12  to 24 hours earlier than the rest of the zones as a result of the showers associated with the cold front. Additionally, the Upper Gunnison River Valley received moderate snow during the latter half of the event.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Generally 2 to 5 inches of snow fell across the area with up to 8 inches measured 2 miles northwest of El Jebel.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong Pacific trough and associated cold front pushed into western Colorado and produced heavy snow across the mountains. Additionally, steep mid-level lapse rates and moderate instability resulted in the development of some convective snow bands which enhanced snowfall rates and accumulations.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Generally 2 to 6 inches of snow fell across the area with up to 7.5 inches measured 4 miles north of Cattle Creek.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The phasing of two systems...a strong low pressure system that lifted across the Four Corners region and a shortwave trough that dove southward through the Great Basin...resulted in moderate to heavy snow over the mountains of western Colorado as well as in some of the northern and central valleys.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Generally 15 to 30 inches of snow fell across the area with locally higher amounts nearing 4 feet. As a result of all the new wet, heavy snow and increased avalanche concern both Lizard Head and Red Mountain Passes closed, with Red Mountain Pass remaining closed indefinitely.

CZ_NAME: WEST JACKSON & WEST GRAND COUNTIES ABOVE 9000 FEET
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong westerly flow aloft ushered a prolonged period of heavy snow and strong winds into the north central mountains of Colorado.  The combination of snow and wind resulted in a high avalanche danger.  An avalanche occurred along Interstate 70 between Frisco and Copper Mountain late in the afternoon on the 3rd.  Several vehicles were trapped by the snow as it swept across the interstate.  Another avalanche occurred near the same area earlier in the day.  No injuries were reported and the vehicles were able to pull out of the snow field.  Large stretch of I-70 now closed through the mountains.  On the 5th, both directions of Interstate 70 between Herman Gulch and Silverthorne were closed for 9 hours for avalanche mitigation work.  One of the avalanches  brought down more snow than expected and covered the westbound lanes with 15 feet of snow and the eastbound lanes with 8 feet.   ||Storm totals for the 3-day event included:  35 inches at Mt Audubon; 32.5 near Mt Zirkel; 27.5 inches near Hoosier Pass, Longs Peak, Loveland Pass; 26 inches near Berthoud Falls, Climax, Glendevey and Silverthorne; 25.5 inches near Frisco; 22.5 inches near Guanella Pass; 20 inches near Cameron Pass; 19 inches at Brainard Lake; 16.5 inches near Ward; 15 inches near Alma, Eldora and Meeker Park.||In the Front Range Foothills, storm totals included:  17 inches near Estes Park; 11 inches near Georgetown and Nederland; 10 inches near Virginia Dale; 8.5 inches near Buckhorn Mountain and Glen Haven.  Along the I-25 corridor and adjacent plains, storm totals included:  8.5 inches near  Fort Collins;  8 inches near Elizabeth and Nunn;  7.5 inches near Loveland; 7 inches near Wellington; with 2 to 6 inches elsewhere.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A supercell thunderstorm developed over the Palmer Divide in northern El Paso County and moved towards Falcon, CO where several storm spotters noted funnel clouds dropping and lifting back up. Around 415 PM MDT, spotter confirmation, with video evidence, shows a small tornado did develop and move towards the east-southeast across Highway 24 near Falcon. NWS Pueblo storm survey team assessed the damage the tornado produced as high end EF-0 damage. An outdoor trailer/RV storage facility and nearby home were damaged by the storm.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Tornado touched down inside Falcon RV Storage facility, flipping several empty trailers/campers and shifting several others off their stabilization blocks. The tornado then lifted and traveled three-quarters of a mile to the southeast side skirting a primary residence off Elbert Road. Damage from the RV Storage facility was laying in the yard of the effected residence. The residence had one of it's exterior walls blown outward as wind got into the house through a set of french doors on the north-northwest side of the house. The remainder of the house was unaffected by the storm which then traveled a little farther southeast before dissipating.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A long duration storm system generated snow, heavy at times over sections of southern Colorado.  Some of the higher reported snow totals with this system included six inches of snow near the communities of Beulah and Buena Vista.  Eight to ten inches of snow was observed near Colorado Springs, Manitou Springs and Peterson Air Force Base.  Twelve inches of snow graced locations near Penrose, while 29 to 30 inches of snow impacted Monarch Pass, Wolf Creek Pass and Leadville respectively.

A landspout formed along a colliding boundary east of Eaton.  The tornado flipped over 100 small cow huts.  None of the calves were injured, although several were chained to the shelters and had to be untangled.  Several feed bags were scattered around as well.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tornado touched down briefly and caused minor damage.

A powerful winter storm system moved across East Central Colorado during the afternoon from west to east.  Pea size hail was reported before the onset of snow.  Not long after the snow starting the high winds developed, causing the visibility to be near zero at times.  Two waves of snow moved across East Central Colorado, the first lasted through the second half of the afternoon.  The second wave began in the late afternoon and lasted into mid evening.  After the snow had ended the high winds continued into the following day.  The peak wind gust was 78 MPH measured at Firstview.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An extremely powerful low pressure system(setting records for lowest pressure measurements in Colorado)developed over southern Colorado and created impressive wind gusts across many southern Colorado locations.  Some of the highest reported wind gusts with this event included 58 to 65 mph wind gusts near the communities of Falcon, Weston, Saguache, Walsenburg, Palmer Lake, Pueblo and Boone.  A 69 mph wind gust was recorded near Aguilar.  70 to 74 mph wind gusts were recorded near Crowley, Weston and Springfield.  A 78 mph wind gust was noted at Blende, while 80 to 84 mph wind gusts were measured near Black Forest and Lamar respectively.  Locations near Truckton realized wind gusts of 85 mph with this event, while Cheraw experienced a wind gust of 89 mph.  Finally, an incredible wind gust of 96 mph was measured in Colorado Springs.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An intense low pressure system developed over southern Colorado on March 13th, producing blizzard conditions at times over El Paso county(focused from later in the morning on March 13th into the afternoon of March 13th).  Unfortunately, 1 direct fatality and 2 indirect fatalities in El Paso county occurred with this intense storm system. ||The direct fatality was due to exposure when a gentleman left his house during the height of the storm to try and start his generator. He became disoriented and couldn't find his home again and spent the night outside where he succumbed to frostbite and hypothermia. Two indirect fatalities were found two weeks after the storm ended. A rapidly intensifying storm system or bomb cyclone brought hurricane strength winds to the northeast plains of Colorado, along with moderate to heavy snowfall.  Peak wind gusts ranged from 60 to 80 mph. Thundersnow produced snowfall rates up to 3 inches per hour just west of Denver.   Two records were established at Denver International Airport.  A record non-thunderstorm wind gust of 80 mph broke the previous record of 63 mph set in Match 13, 2009.  A record low sea level pressure of 979.01 MB broke the previous record of 979.9 MB set on February 25, 1998.  Snowfall amounts ranged from 2 to 6 inches near the base of the foothills, with up to 20 inches in the mountains and foothills.  Along the Palmer Divide east of I-25 and the northeast plains of Colorado, storm totals ranged from 4 to 10 inches.||Widespread outages, multi-vehicle accidents and road closures prompted the governor to declare a state of emergency which activated the Colorado National Guard to assist state and local authorities in rescuing hundreds of stranded motorists.  Disaster declarations included the following counties and cities:  Adams, Arapahoe, Douglas, Elbert counties, as well as the city of Aurora.   Multiple accidents included a 100-car pileup on Interstate 25, from MM 285 to the WY border; with another 100-car accident south of Denver toward Monument. A state trooper was struck and killed on Interstate 76.  He was at the scene of a vehicle off the road near Roggen when another car lost control and struck him.  Road closures included:  Interstate 70 from Denver Airport to Limon, and from Golden to Idaho Springs;  I-25 from South Denver to Monument Hill;  I-76 closed from northeast Denver to Nebraska.||Nearly 1400 flights in and out of Denver International Airport that were canceled due to the blizzard.  Five thousand passengers were stranded at the airport.  The number of people who lost power during the storm totaled 445,000.  Xcel energy called over 500 employees work to restore power with additional 300 out-of-state workers on their way to assist.  Approximately 85,000 people, mostly in the Denver area, were without electricity the following day.  At least 335 vehicles were stuck and abandoned on Interstate 25 near Larkspur.  In Denver, 250 vehicles were abandoned during the storm.  School buses were used to rescue stranded drivers.  At least 33 public school districts were closed on the 13 and 14th.  Warming centers and shelters opened area wide.

A Pacific low and associated cold front pushed across the Great Basin and produced moderate to heavy snow over the mountains of western Colorado, as well as some lower valleys. Additionally, a tightening gradient aloft led to gusty winds at the surface and periods of blowing snow.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An estimated 10 inches of snow fell at the Columbine Pass SNOTEL site.

CZ_NAME: NORTHERN SANGRE DE CRISTO MOUNTAINS ABOVE 11000 FT
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong gradient winds impacted sections of southern Colorado from the afternoon of March 6th into the morning of March 7th.  Some of the higher reported wind gusts with this event included 63 mph winds gusts near Colorado City and Walsenburg and a 68 mph wind gust near Hoehne.
Outflow boundaries associated with collapsing thunderstorms produced severe thunderstorm wind gusts across portions of Western Colorado.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The automated weather station at the Grand Junction Regional Airport (KGJT) measured a peak wind gust of 73 mph from outflow winds associated with a strong thunderstorm.

CZ_NAME: EASTERN LAKE COUNTY / WESTERN MOSQUITO RANGE ABOVE 11000 FT
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An intense low pressure system developed over southern Colorado on March 13th, producing snow and wind-driven snow at times over sections of southern Colorado as well as widespread rain over the southeastern Colorado plains and impressive snow amounts over several higher terrain locations into March 14th. ||Some of the higher reported snow totals with this event included six to nine inches of snow near Cripple Creek, Florissant, and Westcliffe with ten to twelve inches of snow gracing Rosita, San Isabel, Fort Garland and Monarch Pass.  Fourteen inches of snow blanketed Creede while an impressive 52 inches of snow inundated Wolf Creek Pass.

During the early afternoon a line of thunderstorms slowly moved northeast across East Central Colorado.  Hail up to golf ball size fell at Flagler, with the hail reaching a depth of several inches.  The hail was several inches all the way to Siebert as well.  The hail was deep enough to reduce interstate traffic to a crawl.  As the storms exited Flagler a damaging wind gust occurred which blew down many electrical poles a mile or so northwest of town.  This resulted in no power for several hours for parts of Flagler.||During the early evening a second round of thunderstorms moved in from the west-northwest.  As these thunderstorms moved in a wind gust of 70 MPH occurred, blowing a semi on its on I-70.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Sustained winds of 58 MPH were also measured during the peak wind gust.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall amounts ranged from 8 to 14 inches with significant travel impacts over Red Mountain and Lizard Head Passes. Highway 145 was closed for several hours due to multiple accidents and heavy snowfall.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm, producing 1 hail, moved through portions of Baca and Prowers County on the evening of May 1st.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trained spotter reported 1 inch hail fell 12 miles NE of Two Buttes, in Prowers County.

CZ_NAME: LARIMER COUNTY BELOW 6000 FEET / NW WELD COUNTY
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A storm system produced heavy snow in the mountains, with moderate snowfall and strong gusty winds across the lower elevations.   Peak wind gusts generally ranged from 35 to 55 mph and produced near blizzard conditions at times with areas of drifting snow and reduced visibility.  Localized bands of heavier snow spread out across portions of eastern Larimer, Weld and Logan counties as well.  In the mountains and foothills, storm totals included:  15 inches near Winter Park; 13 inches, 6 miles west of Arapahoe Peak; 12 inches near Berthoud Pass and 9 miles south-southeast of Gould; 11 inches near Longs Peak, Meeker Park and Mount Audubon; 10 inches near Eldora, Red Feather Lakes and Silverthorne; 8.5 inches near Estes Park; 8 inches near Climax.  ||Across the urban corridor and adjacent plains, storm totals included:  11.5 inches near Loveland; 10 miles, 4 mile north of Longmont; 9.5 inches near Ponderosa Park; 9 inches near Fort Collins; 8 inches, 15 miles north of Elizabeth; 7.5 inches near Eldorado Springs and Foxfield; 7 inches near Highlands Ranch; 6.5 inches near La Salle and Sterling; 6 inches near Larkspur, Lone Tree, The Pinery and Roxborough Park.   Storm totals elsewhere ranged from 2 to 5 inches.

Early in the afternoon of Monday May 6th, conditions were very favorable for quickly developing deep convection across much of the eastern Colorado plains, including parts of eastern El Paso County. At 145 PM MDT the first of 7 severe thunderstorms warnings was issued. At 201 PM MDT, the Storm Prediction Center placed El Paso, Baca, Kiowa, Bent, Las Animas, Prowers, Crowley, and Otero Counties under Severe Thunderstorm Watch #138 set to expire at 9 PM MDT.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An estimated 69 MPH wind gust reported by public 7 miles south of Springfield, CO.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On the afternoon of Saturday, May 25th, parts of Southern Colorado were highlighted by a Slight risk of severe thunderstorms by the Storm Prediction Center. At 205 PM MDT a Severe Thunderstorm Watch was issued for Baca, Bent, and Prowers Counties across the eastern Colorado plains. By 230 PM MDT the first severe thunderstorm warnings for  damaging hail and winds were issued. Reports of 1 to 1.75 inch hail was reported in Baca County that afternoon before storms moved into Kansas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: One inch hail reported 5 miles north-northeast of Campo, CO.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The afternoon and evening of Sunday, May 26th was a very active severe weather day across the eastern Colorado plains. The region had been outlooked with a Moderate risk of severe thunderstorms and tornadoes. A mesoscale discussion from SPC came out at 1119 AM MDT highlighting much of the eastern plains of Colorado for the need of a Tornado Watch within the next couple of hours. At 1230 PM MDT SPC issued Tornado Watch #253 to include Baca, Bent, Crowley, Kiowa, Las Animas, Otero, and Prowers Counties. Throughout the afternoon and evening hours several supercell thunderstorms developed and moved southwest to northeast through the eastern Colorado plains counties. Several reports of large damaging hail, destructive winds, and a few tornadoes were seen by area SKYWARN spotters and Storm Chasers.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Large hail reported just north of Springfield, CO during a severe thunderstorm.

A mesoscale discussion from SPC came out at 1119 AM MDT highlighting much of the eastern plains of Colorado for the need of a Tornado Watch within the next couple of hours. At 1230 PM MDT SPC issued Tornado Watch #253 to include Baca, Bent, Crowley, Kiowa, Las Animas, Otero, and Prowers Counties. Throughout the afternoon and evening hours several supercell thunderstorms developed and moved southwest to northeast through the eastern Colorado plains counties. Several reports of large damaging hail, destructive winds, and a few tornadoes were seen by area SKYWARN spotters and Storm Chasers.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Large hail reported by trained SKYWARN spotter in northern Colorado Springs.

CZ_NAME: EASTERN LAKE COUNTY / WESTERN MOSQUITO RANGE ABOVE 11000 FT
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A Spring storm system produced snow, heavy at times to portions of southern Colorado.  Six to eight inches of snow graced locations near Texas Creek, Nathrop, Rosita, Buena Vista, Leadville and the Spanish Peaks.  Ten to Twelve inches of snow was measured near the communities of Canon City, Fort Garland and Hillside, while 13 inches of snow covered Maysville and San Isabel.  Finally, 15 inches of snow impacted areas near Westcliffe and Monarch Pass.

Clearing skies in the wake of an exiting winter storm allowed temperatures to drop below freezing across portions of Western Colorado.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: All observations sites within the zone recorded temperatures between 27 and 32 degrees F for several hours.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Clearing skies in the wake of an exiting winter storm allowed temperatures to drop below freezing across portions of Western Colorado.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Over half of the forecast zone experienced temperatures ranging from 30 to 32 degrees F.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The unsettled, progressive weather pattern continued across western Colorado during the month of February as numerous winter disturbances moved through. As a result of the wetter than normal conditions the state of the drought improved across much of the region, most notably in the Four Corners which finally eradicated the remaining exceptional (D4) drought conditions.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The extreme (D3) drought conditions across the southeast portion of the zone improved two categories to the moderate (D1) category. The severe (D2) conditions across the remainder of the zone also improved to the moderate category by the end of the month.

CZ_NAME: BOULDER & JEFFERSON COUNTIES BELOW 6000 FEET / W BROOMFIELD COUNTY
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Bands of heavy snowfall with rates of 2 to 3 inches per hour, struck Denver during the evening commute.  The heaviest snowfall occurred south of a line extending from Arvada to Denver International Airport.  The heavy snow snarled traffic and left many stranded at the airport.  At  Denver International Airport, 8.3 inches of snowfall was observed.  Storm totals elsewhere included:  9 inches in Aurora, 8.5 inches near Centennial; 8 inches near Cherry Creek Reservoir and Littleton, 7.5 inches at Centennial Airport, Commerce City, Highlands Ranch and Lone Tree;  7 inches near Arapahoe Park; 6.5 inches near Buckley AFB, Englewood and Strasburg; 6 inches near Deer Trail, Kiowa and Parker; with 3 to 5 inches in Arvada and Wheat Ridge.

Strong winds impacted portions of southern Colorado during the afternoon of the 3rd with numerous reports of wind gusts in the 60 to 79 mph range recorded near the Air Force Academy in El Paso county, with even a report of a 81 mph wind gust observed 3 miles to the west-southwest of the Air Force Academy.  Elsewhere, a 64 mph wind gust was noted near Fountain, while 65 mph wind gusts were observed near Wetmore and Manitou Springs.  In addition, a large tree knocked down by the strong winds caused damage to a business in Westcliffe.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Two very moist and moderately warm Pacific storms pushed across western Colorado. The second storm had abnormally high moisture content as it was accompanied by an atmospheric river. Favorable southwesterly flow allowed the southern valleys and mountains to receive significant snow by the end of the event, though the northern mountains still saw some impressive accumulations. The excessive amount of moisture paired with the mild origins of the system resulted in low snow ratios which produced wet, heavy snow.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Generally 12 to 27 inches of snow fell across the area with an outlier of an estimated 47 inches at the Tower SNOTEL site.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Two very moist and moderately warm Pacific storms pushed across western Colorado. The second storm had abnormally high moisture content as it was accompanied by an atmospheric river. Favorable southwesterly flow allowed the southern valleys and mountains to receive significant snow by the end of the event, though the northern mountains still saw some impressive accumulations. The excessive amount of moisture paired with the mild origins of the system resulted in low snow ratios which produced wet, heavy snow.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Generally 6 to 12 inches of snow fell across the area. The wet, heavy snow had widespread impact on the Gunnison Basin as it caused several downed power lines and subsequent power outages across the area. One power pole was snapped due to the weight of the snow on the line, and crews could not get to the site with a backhoe and had to have CDOT provide access through the snow with heavier equipment. Numerous observers compared the snow to wet concrete.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Generally 2 to 5 inches of snow fell at the higher elevations with up to 8 inches estimated at Mancos. Winds gusted from 35 to 42 MPH.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Two very moist and moderately warm Pacific storms pushed across western Colorado. The second storm had abnormally high moisture content as it was accompanied by an atmospheric river. Favorable southwesterly flow allowed the southern valleys and mountains to receive significant snow by the end of the event, though the northern mountains still saw some impressive accumulations. The excessive amount of moisture paired with the mild origins of the system resulted in low snow ratios which produced wet, heavy snow.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Generally 6 to 12 inches of snow fell across the area with locally higher amounts up to 16 inches in the surrounding foothills. The heavy, wet snow resulted in power outages with over 6000 outages occurring in the Durango-Pagosa Springs corridor.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A storm system produced heavy snow in the mountains, with moderate snowfall and strong gusty winds across the lower elevations.   Peak wind gusts generally ranged from 35 to 55 mph and produced near blizzard conditions at times with areas of drifting snow and reduced visibility.  Localized bands of heavier snow spread out across portions of eastern Larimer, Weld and Logan counties as well.  In the mountains and foothills, storm totals included:  15 inches near Winter Park; 13 inches, 6 miles west of Arapahoe Peak; 12 inches near Berthoud Pass and 9 miles south-southeast of Gould; 11 inches near Longs Peak, Meeker Park and Mount Audubon; 10 inches near Eldora, Red Feather Lakes and Silverthorne; 8.5 inches near Estes Park; 8 inches near Climax.  ||Across the urban corridor and adjacent plains, storm totals included:  11.5 inches near Loveland; 10 miles, 4 mile north of Longmont; 9.5 inches near Ponderosa Park; 9 inches near Fort Collins; 8 inches, 15 miles north of Elizabeth; 7.5 inches near Eldorado Springs and Foxfield; 7 inches near Highlands Ranch; 6.5 inches near La Salle and Sterling; 6 inches near Larkspur, Lone Tree, The Pinery and Roxborough Park.   Storm totals elsewhere ranged from 2 to 5 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large avalanche released in the Temptation area in the Bear Creek drainage as a back country skier was skinning up the slopes. This avalanche occurred near the town of Telluride, Colorado.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A large avalanche released in the Temptation area in the Bear Creek drainage as a back country skier was skinning up the slopes. The skier was caught in the avalanche and buried under several feet of snow. The avalanche broke around 75 feet on an east-facing slope at an elevation of approximately 11,800 feet. It ran 2000 vertical feet to the valley floor. It deposited debris approximately 1000 feet long, 100 feet wide and 30 feet deep.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong winter storm dropped 1 to 2 feet of new snow across the Central Mountains in Western Colorado on top of an unstable early winter snow pack. Back country skiers triggered an avalanche as they were breaking trail along a steep slope in an area known as Death Pass near Crested Butte.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A pair of back country skiers were skinning up a steep mountain after a recent winter storm brought 1 to 2 feet of new snow to the area. The new snow fell on top of an unstable snow pack from earlier winter storms. The skiers were apparently together in the middle of the slope when the avalanche released. They were carried down an open 37 degree slope and were buried in approximately five feet of snow and debris.  Neither skier survived.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A powerful low pressure system affected much of the southwestern United States from February 20th through the 23rd. This storm was characterized by cold temperatures and very high snowfall totals across the Four Corners region. Over three feet of snow fell in the San Juan Mountains with well over a foot along portions of the Colorado Highway 160 corridor. The northern and central mountains did not see much accumulating snow until the latter half of the storm as a result of the southwesterly flow shadowing areas downwind.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An estimated 34 inches of snow fell over the Columbine Pass SNOTEL site with 4 to 8 inches over the lower elevations.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Moist and moderately fast southwest flow out ahead of and with the passage of a Pacific trough produced significant snow over the mountains of western Colorado. Some of the southern, central and northern valleys also received moderate snowfall. The presence of a strong subtropical jet aloft provided additional support as well as gusty winds at the surface which led to areas of blowing snow and reduced visibility.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Generally 10 to 15 inches of snow fell across the area with some locally higher amounts up to 20 inches. Silverton Mountain Ski Area reported 27 inches. Winds generally gusted between 30 and 45 MPH with a peak gust of 87 MPH at Eagle Mountain.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: At 151 PM MDT the Storm Prediction Center issued a Severe Thunderstorm Watch for Baca, Bent, Prowers, and Kiowa Counties until 900 PM MDT. Golf ball sized hail was reported near Two Buttes.

Storm totals elsewhere ranged from 2 to 5 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An unseasonably moist and cold Pacific trough produced a strong cold front which stalled over portions of northwest Colorado. The quasi-stationary position of this front and the eventual progression east along the Continental Divide produced anomalous snow over some of the northern and central Colorado mountains above 9000 feet. Additionally, instability in the atmosphere allowed snow levels to drop to near 6500 feet but there were no impacts as roads remained wet.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Generally 12 to 20 inches of snow fell above 9000 feet with an estimated 3 to 5 inches falling below that level.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Accelerated snowmelt due to above normal temperatures produced high and fast flow on the Eagle River. A man died from a recreational accident caused by the dangerous water conditions.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A raft carrying five young men down the Eagle River flipped when it hit a tall wave and a hole on the other side of the river near Eagle-Vail. As a result, all five passengers were thrown into the raging river. Four managed to scramble up the bank to safety while one was swept away and eventually passed away.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A significant storm moved across Cheyenne County Colorado and Wallace County Kansas on the evening of June 21, 2019. This storm produced powerful wind gusts and hail ranging from quarter size to tennis ball size. Sporadic wind damage occurred with this storm along with damage associated with wind-driven hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: NWS Storm Survey concluded a significant wind event moved through Cheyenne Wells. Damage consisted of numerous tree limbs broken and down, uplift and overthrow of a metal roof, loss of another deck awning/metal roof, loss of siding to a home, loss of roofing material to a few mobile homes, and a few trees down.

A storm system produced heavy snow in the mountains, with moderate snowfall and strong gusty winds across the lower elevations.   Peak wind gusts generally ranged from 35 to 55 mph and produced near blizzard conditions at times with areas of drifting snow and reduced visibility.  Localized bands of heavier snow spread out across portions of eastern Larimer, Weld and Logan counties as well.  In the mountains and foothills, storm totals included:  15 inches near Winter Park; 13 inches, 6 miles west of Arapahoe Peak; 12 inches near Berthoud Pass and 9 miles south-southeast of Gould; 11 inches near Longs Peak, Meeker Park and Mount Audubon; 10 inches near Eldora, Red Feather Lakes and Silverthorne; 8.5 inches near Estes Park; 8 inches near Climax.  ||Across the urban corridor and adjacent plains, storm totals included:  11.5 inches near Loveland; 10 miles, 4 mile north of Longmont; 9.5 inches near Ponderosa Park; 9 inches near Fort Collins; 8 inches, 15 miles north of Elizabeth; 7.5 inches near Eldorado Springs and Foxfield; 7 inches near Highlands Ranch; 6.5 inches near La Salle and Sterling; 6 inches near Larkspur, Lone Tree, The Pinery and Roxborough Park.   Storm totals elsewhere ranged from 2 to 5 inches.

A Flash Flood Watch was issued for the Sangre de Cristo range, Spanish Peaks, and the Rato Mesa on Tuesday, June 4, 2019, including the highly susceptible Spring burn scar. Deep, moist upslope flow over the area brought the possibility of locally heavy rain at times over and near the eastern mountains.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Huerfano County Emergency Manager reported water flowing over CR 442 just west of CR 441 making the road impassable.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very active severe weather day with flash flooding occurred much of the Front Range and eastern Colorado plains counties.||At 235 PM MDT the Storm Prediction Center in Norman, OK issued Severe Thunderstorm Watch #533 for Baca, Bent, Crowley, El Paso, Kiowa, Las Animas, Otero, Prowers, and Pueblo Counties valid until 11 PM MDT. ||By 430 PM MDT Flash Flood and Severe Thunderstorm Warnings were issued for parts of El Paso County, mainly the Colorado Springs vicinity. The stronger activity began to spread to the east and south throughout the late afternoon and evening hours. Several reports of funnel clouds just east of downtown Colorado Springs were reported along with large damaging hail, damaging wind gusts, wind damage, and flash flooding. ||One particular storm that developed just north of Colorado Springs tracked southeast over the course of 2 hours producing flash flooding in Manitou Springs, large hail in downtown Colorado Springs, funnel clouds near Black Forest, and wind damage near Schreiver AFB.||Farther south in Pueblo County, a severe thunderstorm developed in the foothills of the Wet Mountains and then moved towards Pueblo West and Pueblo. Along the track of the storm numerous intersections in Pueblo were closed due to high water from flash flooding. A few reports of large hail occurred on the west side of the city and a 62 MPH was recorded at the Pueblo County Memorial Airport.||This storm continued to move towards the east producing a few spotty reports of wind damage and severe wind gusts in Crowley, Otero, and Prowers Counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Nearly 8 inches of muddy water flowing down area roadways near Manitou Springs.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Accelerated snowmelt due to above normal temperatures produced high and fast flow on the Rio Grande River. One women died after being swept into the dangerous water conditions in Pole Creek.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A woman was swept into Pole Creek while trying to cross the stream with her husband in a Jeep. She was missing for almost a month before her body was found along the Rio Grande River on July 25th.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A woman was kayaking in Blue Mesa Reservoir near the Gunnison River inlet and passed away.

During the afternoon hours of July 1st very heavy monsoonal thunderstorms developed over the high terrain of the Sawatch, Wet, and Sangre de Cristo mountain ranges and then drifted east towards the southern Colorado plains. ||Flash flooding and debris flows were reported on the Spring burn scar in both Costilla and Huerfano Counties. Heavy rain and flash flooding also occurred on the east side of Pueblo closing several local roads.||The severe storms that produced flooding in Pueblo also packed a punch with strong damaging winds. Several reports of broken tree branches near the county courthouse were reported. Wind gusted over 60 MPH with these storms as well.||One person, with unknown injuries, was struct by lightning in Canon City during a heavy rain event.||Out in Baca County, heavy rain lead to some minor street flooding with wind gusts reported over 60 MPH.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Sheriffs deputy reported 3-4 feet of water  over Huerfano County road 443.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: At 155 PM MDT the Storm Prediction Center issued a Severe Thunderstorm Watch #321 including much of the Front Range of Colorado and the eastern Colorado plains, valid until 900 PM MDT. Around 200 PM MDT, the forecast office in Pueblo began issuing Severe Thunderstorm and Tornado Warnings for several supercells that developed and moved eastward throughout the afternoon hours. Several reports of large damaging hail were relayed to the office as the supercells tracked through Colorado Springs and parts of Baca County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Large hail fell across the northern part of Colorado Springs.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the afternoon of July 2, 2019, heavy rain developed near San Luis, CO and slowly moved towards the Spring burn scar. Radar estimates 1 to 1.5 inches of rain fell within an hour across the center portion of the burn scar. Debris flows and mudslides were reported by the Huerfano County Emergency Manager.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flash flooding and debris-mud flows, from Indian Creek, across portions of CR 421 in and just north of Sulphur Springs was reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A supercell thunderstorm developed in northern Teller County and then proceeded to move east-southeast in El Paso County. By mid-afternoon on Independence Day, mother nature started her own fireworks with large hailstones falling from Monument to Black Forest.
